channel.title: Test WordPress Blog
channel.description: Generated by WordPress; provides full text in feed.

item.title: The Habit of Returning
item.dc:creator: WP Author
item.description: A short summary of the post.
item.content:encoded:
Most of what I now think of as my own habits started as imitations. The imitation was conscious in some cases — I copied a sentence shape from a writer I admired and noticed, months later, that the shape had become mine in a way the original sentence had not — and unconscious in others, the way you absorb the cadence of the people you spend time with without remembering having decided to.

The interesting question is whether the conscious imitations or the unconscious ones leave a deeper mark. My intuition is that the conscious ones do, because the act of paying attention to a shape is itself a habit-forming move. You cannot unsee what you have spent a week trying to write. The unconscious imitations, by contrast, can be peeled off with a different week's exposure to a different writer; they are surface in a way the chosen ones are not.

This is an argument for choosing one's models, and for choosing them slowly. The instinct to consume a great deal of variety, to rotate through influences the way one rotates through outfits, is one I have followed and now mistrust. The writers who have actually changed me, in the sense that I would notice their absence in my own prose if they vanished from my reading life, are a small group, and I returned to each of them many times before I could feel them working on me at the level beneath the level I could see.

So: who do you return to, and what is the shape of their return on you? The two questions, taken together, will tell you what your prose is made of, more reliably than any account you can give of your own intentions.
